notes.note: Caecina was an author as well as Cicero's friend; this is likely his inflammatory pamphlet on the civil war (the Liber Querelarum or a related work), which he had sent for Cicero's safekeeping. The 'most careful guard' hints at the political danger of the text under Caesar's dominance.

notes.anchor: this fortune of yours, and of the others, cannot be lasting
notes.note: Caecina was in exile as a Pompeian after the Battle of Thapsus (46 BC); Cicero is consoling him with the argument that Caesar's victors cannot indefinitely punish good men who fought on the losing side.

notes.anchor: this man in whose power we stand
notes.note: Caesar, named obliquely in the characteristic Ciceronian periphrasis that was both prudent and revealing: the phrase 'in whose power' (in potestate) acknowledges the revolution without naming it. In 46 BC Caesar was dictator for the second time.

notes.anchor: the talent of yours by which he is delighted
notes.note: Caesar is portrayed as simultaneously admiring Caecina's literary talent and resenting the use to which it had been put against him; the paradox drives Cicero's argument that the same talent will ultimately secure Caecina's pardon.
